title: 5.2.2 Graph Traversal and DFS Algorithm
subtitle: Depth-first search process and extension-ready visualization/code/practice

content.introduction: Depth-First Search (DFS) explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, making it effective for structural analysis.

sections.title: 5.2.2.1 Key Ideas and Model
sections.content: Define DFS traversal order, visited marking, and recursion/stack equivalence to avoid repeated exploration.
examples: Detect connectivity in an undirected graph
Use visited set to prevent infinite loops

sections.title: 5.2.2.2 Representation and Workflow
sections.content: Describe recursive and iterative DFS workflows for connected and disconnected graphs.
examples: Trace DFS from a start node on adjacency list
Implement iterative DFS with explicit stack
Run full DFS forest for disconnected graphs

sections.title: 5.2.2.3 Complexity and Practical Guidance
sections.content: DFS runs in O(V+E), with stack depth linked to path length. Discuss cycle handling and traversal order effects.
examples: Estimate recursion depth on deep graphs
Compare DFS with BFS for shortest path in unweighted graphs
Use DFS for topological and component-style analyses

keyTakeaways: Implement both recursive and iterative forms
Handle cycles and disconnected graphs safely
Use DFS in graph-structure analysis tasks
